news_summary: Breakthrough research has successfully extracted ancient human DNA from cave art, offering new insights into early human history and prehistoric culture.
news_summary_fr: Des recherches révolutionnaires ont permis d'extraire avec succès de l'ADN humain ancien à partir d'œuvres d'art rupestre, offrant ainsi un nouvel éclairage sur l'histoire des premiers humains et la culture préhistorique.
news_summary_es: Una investigación pionera ha logrado extraer ADN humano antiguo del arte rupestre, lo que ofrece nuevos conocimientos sobre la historia de los primeros seres humanos y la cultura prehistórica.

articles.title: Ancient human DNA found on cave art for the first time
articles.summary: DNA from ancient humans has been found on a prehistoric cave painting and on cave walls, demonstrating the potential to one day identify individual artists and resolve the debate over Neanderthals' artistic abilities
articles.content:
DNA from ancient humans has been found on a prehistoric cave painting and on cave walls, demonstrating the potential to one day identify individual artists and resolve the debate over Neanderthals' artistic abilities

A sample is collected from a rock art figure in Tebellín, Spain ABAMIA ARKEOS-ALBERTO MARTÍNEZ VILLA

Ancient human DNA can survive on cave walls and rock art for thousands of years, a study of caves in Spain and Portugal has found. This opens up new ways to understand prehistoric humans and answer questions about whether Neanderthals painted on cave walls too.

“It’s the start of a new era,” says Genevieve von Petzinger at University of the Witwatersrand, Johannesburg, South Africa. “This gives us the potential to meet the actual artists, the individual who did this art. It’s extraordinary.”

Between 2022 and 2025, a team of researchers from the First Art project, which focuses on dating the earliest cave art, took samples from 11 caves around Spain and Portugal containing rock art – mainly graphic images such as triangles, dots and hand stencils made using red ochre paint, which are thought to be the oldest forms of cave drawings. The researchers took tiny shavings of paint or removed a layer of calcite mineral that forms on cave walls by precipitation from water.

Cave art is often created by spitting paint, or applied using hands and fingers, so the researchers tested whether any DNA from the artists had been preserved. We have known for a decade that ancient human DNA can be preserved in the sediment on cave floors, but this genetic material had never previously been discovered on the walls.

This has now changed, with the discovery of ancient human DNA in some red markings in the Escoural Cave in Portugal that resemble a semicolon.

Subscriber-only newsletter Sign up to Our Human Story Each month, Michael Marshall unearths the latest news and ideas about ancient humans, evolution, archaeology and more. Sign up to newsletter

“That was a happy surprise,” says Alba Bossoms Mesa at the Max Planck Institute for Evolutionary Anthropology in Leipzig, Germany. This is the first time ancient human DNA has been found on cave walls. However, we can’t yet be certain that the DNA is from the person who created the art, she says. “It could be from someone who touched the art later, or from someone who just sneezed.”

However, this opens the door to one day being able to identify the people who made the drawings. “It is as though the cave walls have become the pages of a blank book that, little by little, we will be able to fill with new discoveries,” says Hipólito Collado Giraldo, an archaeologist at the regional government of Extremadura, Spain.

Pigment samples were collected from the ceiling of Altamira cave in Spain Matthias Meyer

Another big surprise came when the researchers took samples from areas of the cave walls with no art, intended as controls, and found ancient human DNA in some of them too, presumably left over by prehistoric visitors to the cave who touched the walls. “We were absolutely astonished,” says Collado Giraldo. This means that cave walls could be a treasure trove of information about the ancient humans who visited them, even where there are no cave drawings or archaeological artefacts.

What’s more, the study showed that the DNA on the walls of the Escoural cave most likely came from direct contact with ancient humans, rather than from sediments from the cave that become deposited on the walls. This is because when human DNA is found in sediment, it is mixed with that of different animals, whereas the genetic material on the Escoural cave wall was exclusively human.

The cave wall DNA also revealed crucial details about the ancient humans it came from. Three of the samples were mainly from females, whereas the fourth was predominantly male. The genetic profile was most closely matched to a population known as western hunter-gatherers who date from around 5200 to 17,000 years ago.

There was not enough DNA recovered to carry out exact dating, but we know that the Escoural cave was sealed off between 4000 and 5000 years ago, so the DNA is probably older than that.

But this study is just the start. Earlier this month, First Art researchers, including von Petzinger and Collado Giraldo, carried out extensive sampling at a number of other caves in Spain. This included Nerja and Ardales caves that contain art attributed to Neanderthals – though this is the subject of much debate. “One question that I would really love to answer… is whether Neanderthals were making art,” says Bossoms Mesa.

DNA from cave walls could open up new ways of understanding ancient humans and the drawings they made, says Francesco d’Errico at the University of Bordeaux in France, who was not involved with the study. “Were the artists men or women or both? Were animal [drawings] from the same panel made by a single artist? Can we find Neanderthal DNA [in the very old paintings in the Iberian peninsula] or Denisovan DNA in hand stencils found in Indonesia? The potential is huge.”

However, ancient DNA was only found in one of the 24 rock art panels sampled, suggesting that preservation might be the exception rather than the norm. “The success rate is very low right now,” says Bossoms Mesa. This is likely to improve as the researchers hone their ability to extract tiny amounts of DNA from cave samples.

Collado Giraldo is excited about the prospect of discovering invaluable information without the need to carry out excavations, which are inherently destructive. “Excavation inevitably removes part of the archaeological record,” he says. “However, this new discovery offers us the opportunity to uncover and reconstruct entirely new stories without excavation – stories that will help us better understand the people and societies of the past.”
articles.reasoning: The discovery of ancient human DNA on cave walls and prehistoric art represents a significant scientific breakthrough with broad implications for understanding human history and prehistoric art. It opens new, non-destructive methods to study ancient humans and potentially identify individual artists, including Neanderthals, which is a major advancement in archaeology and genetics. The article provides detailed context about the research, its methods, and its potential impact, fulfilling the criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Ancient human DNA found on cave paintings for the first time
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied cave paintings to learn about early humans. These paintings, often made with red ochre paint, show simple shapes and handprints. Until now, no one had found ancient human DNA on the walls or the art itself.

**What happened** – Researchers from Spain and Portugal collected tiny paint samples and mineral layers from 11 caves between 2022 and 2025. They discovered ancient human DNA on some of the red markings in the Escoural Cave in Portugal. DNA was also found on cave walls without art, likely left by visitors touching the surfaces long ago.

**Impact** – This is the first time ancient human DNA has been found on cave walls and paintings. It means scientists might one day identify the individual artists who made the drawings, including possibly Neanderthals. The DNA also showed details like the sex of some ancient people and linked them to known hunter-gatherer groups. This new method offers a way to learn about ancient humans without digging up caves.

**What's next step** – Researchers will continue sampling other caves, including ones with art possibly made by Neanderthals. They hope to improve DNA detection and answer questions like who the artists were and whether Neanderthals created some of the oldest cave art.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Finding ancient human DNA on cave paintings opens a new path to discovering who made prehistoric art and learning more about our ancient ancestors.
articles.personality_title_fr: ADN humain ancien découvert sur des peintures rupestres pour la première fois
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les peintures rupestres pour mieux comprendre les premiers humains. Ces peintures, souvent réalisées avec de l'ocre rouge, montrent des formes simples et des empreintes de mains. Jusqu'à présent, aucun ADN humain ancien n'avait été trouvé sur les parois ou les œuvres.

**Ce qui s'est passé** – Entre 2022 et 2025, des chercheurs d'Espagne et du Portugal ont prélevé de minuscules échantillons de peinture et de couches minérales dans 11 grottes. Ils ont découvert de l'ADN humain ancien sur certaines marques rouges dans la grotte d'Escoural au Portugal. De l'ADN a également été trouvé sur des murs sans peinture, probablement laissé par des visiteurs anciens qui touchaient les surfaces.

**Impact** – C'est la première fois qu'un ADN humain ancien est trouvé sur des murs et des peintures rupestres. Cela signifie que les scientifiques pourraient un jour identifier les artistes individuels ayant réalisé ces dessins, y compris peut-être des Néandertaliens. L'ADN a aussi révélé des détails comme le sexe de certains anciens humains et leur lien avec des groupes de chasseurs-cueilleurs connus. Cette nouvelle méthode permet d'en apprendre plus sans fouiller les grottes.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont continuer à prélever des échantillons dans d'autres grottes, notamment celles contenant des peintures attribuées aux Néandertaliens. Ils espèrent améliorer la détection d'ADN et répondre à des questions sur les artistes et l'origine des plus anciennes œuvres.

**Phrase clé** – La découverte d'ADN humain ancien sur des peintures rupestres ouvre une nouvelle voie pour découvrir qui a créé l'art préhistorique et mieux connaître nos ancêtres.
articles.personality_title_es: ADN humano antiguo encontrado por primera vez en pinturas rupestres
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado las pinturas rupestres durante mucho tiempo para aprender sobre los primeros humanos. Estas pinturas, hechas con pintura de ocre rojo, muestran formas simples y manos. Hasta ahora, no se había encontrado ADN humano antiguo en las paredes ni en el arte.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores de España y Portugal tomaron pequeñas muestras de pintura y capas minerales de 11 cuevas entre 2022 y 2025. Descubrieron ADN humano antiguo en algunas marcas rojas en la cueva de Escoural en Portugal. También encontraron ADN en paredes sin arte, probablemente dejado por visitantes que tocaron las paredes hace mucho tiempo.

**Impacto** – Es la primera vez que se encuentra ADN humano antiguo en paredes y pinturas rupestres. Esto significa que los científicos podrían identificar a los artistas que hicieron los dibujos, incluyendo posiblemente a los neandertales. El ADN mostró detalles como el sexo de algunas personas antiguas y su relación con grupos de cazadores-recolectores conocidos. Este método ofrece una forma de aprender sobre los antiguos sin excavar.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores seguirán tomando muestras en otras cuevas, incluidas las que tienen arte atribuido a neandertales. Esperan mejorar la detección de ADN y responder preguntas sobre quiénes fueron los artistas y si los neandertales crearon algunas de las obras más antiguas.

**Frase clave** – Encontrar ADN humano antiguo en pinturas rupestres abre un nuevo camino para descubrir quién hizo el arte prehistórico y aprender más sobre nuestros antepasados.
articles.image_prompt: A warm, detailed painting of an ancient cave interior softly illuminated by natural light, featuring symbolic red ochre hand stencils and geometric shapes like triangles and dots on textured stone walls, with delicate, translucent strands of DNA spiraling gently over the painted surfaces, evoking a connection between prehistoric artists and their preserved genetic legacy.
